title: Switch from top to sleep to make sure tests passes
body:
This test is regularly flaking, changing from top to sleep to see
if this fixes the issue.  Perhaps one of the top containers is exiting
for some reason.

Signed-off-by: Daniel J Walsh <dwalsh@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

`[Fail] Podman top [It] podman pod top on pod with containers in different namespace `

Still flaking

Thanks @rhatdan and @baude @mheon @vrothberg.

    There seems to be others that could have this issue.

Yet another reason to actually find and fix the fundamental problem here.   I really like your theory of ``podmanTest.Podman`` wait() vs podman pod back-grounding.  Is there a way we can artificially exacerbate that problem and see if it causes the same results to occur?

[I think Ed's thinking along the correct lines here](https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/2548#issuecomment-470143999) using the [T functions](https://golang.org/pkg/testing/#T) to catch this problem in the act, and proove the underlying condition.  Otherwise I think adding strategic sleeps is fine for debugging purposes, but we should not rely on them.
I'm wondering about a test only function that would mimic Wait but instead of waiting for the containers to exit, we would be waiting for the containers to all hit a certain status?

> I'm wondering about a test only function that would mimic Wait but instead of waiting for the containers to exit, we would be waiting for the containers to all hit a certain status?

In general to test a component of software, it either has to guarantee completion, or the test has to prove the expected state.  So, having a short-cut (as you suggest) is generally **very** beneficial for testing, especially if it's applicable to many tests.  If it only helps for this one test, then it's probably not worthwhile.
Closing this PR since @baude has attempting to fix it with a different PR>
